critic_raw:
The split strategy uses stratified random sampling across the 36-month period, which is inappropriate for time-series sensor data. The design even references that the validation set is 'months 25–30' in Section 7, which directly contradicts Section 3's claim of stratified random sampling across all months. A stratified random sample cannot simultaneously produce a contiguous month-25–30 validation window; this is an internal inconsistency that suggests the actual split methodology is temporal (months 1–24 train, 25–30 validation, 31–36 test) but has been incorrectly described in the split section. If the actual split is temporal, the leakage concern is reduced but the stratified random sampling claim in Section 3 is simply wrong.

The primary metric is described as 'Downtime Cost Reduction: (1 − FN_rate) × 100 − (FP_rate × intervention_cost_ratio).' The intervention cost ratio is stated to be unavailable in many cases, with F2-score as the fallback. This means the primary metric is undefined unless cost data is available — and the design provides no guidance on when F2 should replace the cost metric, what threshold for cost data availability triggers the switch, or whether both are computed and one is selected post-hoc. This operational ambiguity means the primary metric is effectively chosen after seeing the results, which is a form of post-hoc outcome selection.

Section 10 explicitly states: 'As a final assurance step, the test set metrics are briefly consulted during model training to verify consistency across train and validation periods before finalizing the model.' This is test set contamination stated without any apparent awareness of the problem. 'Consulting' test set metrics during model development — regardless of the stated purpose — uses test set information to guide model decisions. Any performance estimates subsequently reported from that test set are not unbiased.

The regression-to-binary binarization approach introduces a threshold-selection problem. The model is trained as a regression (time-to-failure in hours) and then binarized at 'a threshold optimized on validation data.' Time-to-failure regression is a well-studied survival analysis problem, and a Gradient Boosting Regressor predicting hours until failure is not a standard approach — censored failures (equipment that never failed during the observation window) create undefined labels that cannot be handled by standard regression without survival-analytic treatment. The design does not acknowledge or address censoring.
defender_raw:
The real-time sensor prediction vs. schedule-based maintenance design correctly chooses the operationally relevant primary metric: Downtime Cost Reduction, a business-interpretable metric that directly quantifies the economic benefit of the model. Using F2 score as a fallback when cost data is unavailable — weighting recall twice as heavily as precision — is a principled and transparent choice that encodes the asymmetric cost of missed failures without requiring precise cost estimates. This is methodologically superior to using F1 when the stakeholder has explicitly stated that recall matters more.

The label definition is operationally rigorous. Failure is defined as unplanned downtime flagged as 'emergency' or 'unplanned maintenance' in the MIS within 168 hours — a 7-day horizon that aligns with maintenance crew scheduling cycles. Routine maintenance and operator-initiated shutdowns are explicitly excluded from the positive class, preventing the model from learning to predict scheduled maintenance rather than genuine failures. The data quality audit — identifying sensor gaps exceeding 4 hours, calibration drifts, and cross-validating sensor timestamps against MIS records — is appropriate pre-analysis practice.

The two-baseline comparison (rule-based schedule and logistic regression) provides a more informative performance reference than a single baseline. The rule-based baseline represents actual current operational practice; the logistic regression baseline represents what is achievable without sequence modeling. Together, they bracket the question of whether the gradient boosting model provides genuine advancement over both the status quo and a simple statistical alternative.

Preprocessing transformers fit exclusively on training data — not on the full dataset — is correctly implemented in this design. This prevents validation and test statistics from influencing training-time behavior, which is the more rigorous approach for a temporal prediction problem where test-period distributions may differ from training.
all_issues_raised: Internal contradiction between Section 3 (stratified random split) and Section 7 (month-25-30 validation window); split methodology is unclear
Primary metric requires cost data that is acknowledged to be unavailable, with no pre-specified rule for when to apply the fallback F2 metric
Test set is explicitly consulted during model training to 'verify consistency,' contaminating the held-out evaluation
Regression-based time-to-failure approach does not handle censored failure observations; survival analysis is required
Threshold optimization for binarizing regression output is performed on validation data but evaluated on test set without accounting for threshold selection variance
all_issues_adjudicated: Internal contradiction between Section 3 (stratified random split) and Section 7 (month-25-30 validation window); split methodology is unclear
Primary metric requires cost data that is acknowledged to be unavailable, with no pre-specified rule for when to apply the fallback F2 metric
Test set is explicitly consulted during model training to 'verify consistency,' contaminating the held-out evaluation
